questions.q: In which year was the Film Finance Corporation established by the Government of India?
questions.explanation: The Film Finance Corporation was set up by the Government of India in 1960 to support the film industry.

questions.q: Which body established the Film Finance Corporation in 1960?
questions.explanation: The Film Finance Corporation was a government initiative created by the Government of India.

questions.q: What was the primary form of support the Film Finance Corporation provided to filmmakers?
questions.explanation: The Film Finance Corporation provided low-interest loans to fund films, especially non-commercial ventures.

questions.q: Into which larger organisation did the Film Finance Corporation merge in 1980?
questions.explanation: In 1980 the Film Finance Corporation merged with another body to form the National Film Development Corporation (NFDC).

questions.q: Which 1969 Mrinal Sen film was funded with a loan from the Film Finance Corporation?
questions.explanation: Bhuvan Shome (1969), directed by Mrinal Sen, was financed by the Film Finance Corporation.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation is most associated with funding which kind of films?
questions.explanation: The Corporation chiefly funded experimental, low-budget, non-commercial films rather than mainstream blockbusters.

questions.q: Which 1969 Basu Chatterjee film, an adaptation of a Rajendra Yadav novel, was backed by the Film Finance Corporation?
questions.explanation: Sara Akash (1969), Basu Chatterjee's directorial debut, was funded by the Film Finance Corporation.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation is widely credited with helping launch which film movement in India?
questions.explanation: By funding low-budget art films, the Corporation helped launch the Indian New Wave, also called Parallel cinema.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation operated as which type of organisation?
questions.explanation: The Film Finance Corporation was a public-sector body run by the Government of India.

questions.q: Roughly how long did the Film Finance Corporation exist before merging into the NFDC?
questions.explanation: Founded in 1960 and merged in 1980, the Film Finance Corporation existed for about 20 years.

questions.q: The success of which 1969 Film Finance Corporation-funded debut is often cited as the start of the Indian New Wave?
questions.explanation: Mrinal Sen's Bhuvan Shome (1969), funded by the Corporation, is widely seen as a key starting point of the Indian New Wave.

questions.q: Which director made his feature debut with Sara Akash (1969), financed by the Film Finance Corporation?
questions.explanation: Sara Akash (1969) marked the directorial debut of Basu Chatterjee and was funded by the Corporation.

questions.q: The 1980 merger that created the NFDC combined the Film Finance Corporation with which organisation?
options: The Indian Motion Picture Producers' Association
The Indian Motion Picture Export Corporation
questions.explanation: The Film Finance Corporation merged with the Indian Motion Picture Export Corporation in 1980 to form the NFDC.

questions.q: In which Indian city was the Film Finance Corporation headquartered?
questions.explanation: The Film Finance Corporation, like its successor the NFDC, was headquartered in Bombay (Mumbai).

questions.q: Which of these directors received Film Finance Corporation backing for an early feature in the late 1960s or early 1970s?
questions.explanation: Mrinal Sen received Film Finance Corporation backing for Bhuvan Shome (1969).

questions.q: What did the Film Finance Corporation's lending policy emphasise that distinguished it from ordinary commercial financiers?
options: Backing artistic and experimental films over guaranteed profit
questions.explanation: The Corporation deliberately backed artistic and experimental films rather than judging projects only by commercial profit.

questions.q: Bhuvan Shome (1969), funded by the Film Finance Corporation, was directed by which filmmaker?
questions.explanation: Bhuvan Shome (1969) was directed by Mrinal Sen with finance from the Film Finance Corporation.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation's funding model is best described as supporting films that were considered what at the time?
questions.explanation: The Corporation took on projects regarded as financially risky and non-commercial that mainstream financiers avoided.

questions.q: Which decade saw the Film Finance Corporation fund the early New Wave films like Bhuvan Shome and Sara Akash?
questions.explanation: Both Bhuvan Shome and Sara Akash were released in 1969, during the 1960s, with Corporation funding.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation's successor, the NFDC, continued which broad mission inherited from it?
options: Producing and financing quality Indian cinema
questions.explanation: The NFDC carried forward the Corporation's mission of financing and promoting quality Indian cinema.

questions.q: Why is the Film Finance Corporation regarded as a landmark in Indian film history?
options: It built India's first film studio
It introduced sound to Indian films
It enabled a wave of art-house films that commercial finance would not fund
It produced the first Indian colour film
questions.explanation: The Corporation is remembered for enabling art-house and experimental films that commercial financiers would not back.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation supported films in which broad category of Indian cinema?
questions.explanation: The Corporation's loans primarily supported Parallel (art) cinema rather than mainstream masala films.

questions.q: Bhuvan Shome (1969), funded by the Film Finance Corporation, featured the voice of which then-newcomer in its narration?
questions.explanation: A young Amitabh Bachchan provided narration for Bhuvan Shome (1969), one of his earliest film associations.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation came under criticism in the 1970s mainly because of what problem with its loans?
options: Loans were too large for any film
Many funded films were never released and loans went unrecovered
questions.explanation: The Corporation was criticised because many of its experimental films had poor commercial release and loans often went unrecovered.

questions.q: Under which Union ministry did the Film Finance Corporation operate as a government enterprise?
questions.explanation: The Film Finance Corporation was a public-sector undertaking under the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, which also oversaw its successor, the NFDC.

questions.q: Mrinal Sen's Bhuvan Shome (1969), backed by the Film Finance Corporation, was made primarily in which language?
questions.explanation: Though Mrinal Sen was a Bengali filmmaker, Bhuvan Shome (1969) was made in Hindi.

questions.q: Sara Akash (1969), an early Film Finance Corporation-funded film, was adapted from a novel by which Hindi author?
questions.explanation: Basu Chatterjee's Sara Akash (1969), funded by the Corporation, was adapted from the Hindi novel of the same name by Rajendra Yadav.

questions.q: Bhuvan Shome (1969), financed by the Film Finance Corporation, won the National Film Award for Best Feature Film in which language category?
questions.explanation: Bhuvan Shome won the National Film Award for Best Feature Film in Hindi, validating the Corporation's art-cinema funding.

questions.q: After the 1980 merger, the entity that absorbed the Film Finance Corporation went on to become best known by which acronym?
questions.explanation: The merged entity became the National Film Development Corporation, universally known by the acronym NFDC.

questions.q: The Film Finance Corporation's legacy is most directly visible today through which continuing institution?
questions.explanation: The Corporation's legacy continues through the NFDC, which absorbed it in 1980 and still finances Indian films.
